reasoning: All five pass the mechanical gate, but a reviewer probe found one real defect: Candidate A's is_lost(state) returns remaining_water <= 0 without the 'and not is_won(state)' guard, so a fully-revealed word at zero water is misclassified as a loss (is_won and is_lost both True) — a contract violation since the task defines loss as 'water at 0 BEFORE the win.' A's bug is masked inside apply_guess (win is checked first) and A's own suite never calls is_lost on a won state, which is exactly why it slipped the gate; it would surface the moment task-002's UI calls the standalone predicate. B, C, D, and E all guard is_lost correctly and all 31 probe assertions pass for each. Among the four correct candidates, D wins: it has the most rigorous and most readable suite (80 tests) covering precisely the edges that matter — explicit immutability checks, single-letter words, multi-occurrence reveal, length-window stress, message-template formatting, and the very test A lacked (test_is_lost_false_when_won, win-on-last-water precedence) — over clean immutable transitions, exact-string message assertions against imported constants, and spec-faithful win/loss text with {word} interpolation. Runner-up flaws: E ships the field's only true end-to-end win through the real factory but its MSG_LOSS drops SPEC 6.7's 'ran out of water' line, the weakest loss-text fidelity; C is the broadest (84) but leans on substring and game.MSG_* attribute assertions rather than imported names, giving weaker message-rot protection and it skips .upper() in new_game; B is the leanest and best-factored (the words_for split, best message hygiene) but covers the fewest edges. A is disqualified outright by the is_lost defect.
